headline: Indonesian Wildlife Project Turns Phone Cameras Into Conservation Tools
meta_description: Local photographers in Borneo earn money documenting wildlife on a mobile app, creating a powerful incentive for forest conservation.
summary_short: A conservation organization in Indonesian Borneo is paying local people to photograph wildlife and upload sightings through an app, creating nearly 175,000 records in on…
body:
In Indonesian Borneo, a conservation organization called KehatiKu has found an elegant way to protect orangutans and other wildlife: turn smartphones into tools for scientific observation. Local people can now photograph animals they encounter and upload the images through a dedicated app, earning money for their contributions.

The financial incentive has proven effective. Those who photograph orangutans can earn about $6 per image, while sightings of more common species also qualify for payment. In its first year, the project collected approximately 175,000 wildlife records—a remarkable dataset that helps researchers understand animal populations and behavior across the region.

By linking conservation directly to income, the program addresses a fundamental challenge: how to make protecting forests more valuable than clearing them. When local communities become active participants in wildlife monitoring and benefit financially from doing so, they gain a stake in the forest's survival. The approach transforms everyday people into citizen scientists, turning a camera into an instrument of hope for one of our planet's most endangered species.
